# Scrambled Eggs on Toast вЂ“ Shopping List

## Essential Ingredients
- [ ] Eggs (4 large)
- [ ] Bread (sliced, for toasting)
- [ ] Butter (for cooking & toast)
- [ ] Salt
- [ ] Black pepper

## Optional Extras
- [ ] Milk or cream (splash, for fluffier eggs)
- [ ] Chives or parsley (fresh, for garnish)
- [ ] Cheese (cheddar or your preference, for melting in)

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an agent. Each agent has instructions - the goal of the agent.
  The agent plan shows the current progress of the agent.
  Agent has a number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When creating a new artifact, always add it to the agent using the add-artifact function.
  
  <agent id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTG8" name="Expense tracking">
    <instructions>
      Keep a list of expenses in a markdown document (create artifact "Expenses").
  Process incoming emails, add the relevant ones to the list.
  
  Format:
  
  ## Expenses
  - Flight to London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
  - Hotel in London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100

    <plan>
      
    </plan>

  </agent>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an agent. Each agent has instructions - the goal of the agent.
  The agent plan shows the current progress of the agent.
  Agent has a number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When creating a new artifact, always add it to the agent using the add-artifact function.
  
  <agent id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTG8" name="Expense tracking">
    <instructions>
      Keep a list of expenses in a markdown document (create artifact "Expenses").
  Process incoming emails, add the relevant ones to the list.
  
  Format:
  
  ## Expenses
  - Flight to London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
  - Hotel in London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
    </instructions>
    <plan>
      
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:@:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTJR">
        Expenses
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </agent>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You read, write & create markdown documents.
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

</object>

I think it's reasonable to include them as expenses since they're travel-related purchases made during the trip.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an agent. Each agent has instructions - the goal of the agent.
  The agent plan shows the current progress of the agent.
  Agent has a number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When creating a new artifact, always add it to the agent using the add-artifact function.
  
  <agent id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTG8" name="Expense tracking">
    <instructions>
      Keep a list of expenses in a markdown document (create artifact "Expenses").
  Process incoming emails, add the relevant ones to the list.
  
  Format:
  
  ## Expenses
  - Flight to London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
  - Hotel in London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
    </instructions>
    <plan>
      
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:@:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTJR">
        Expenses

    </artifacts>
  </agent>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You read, write & create markdown documents.
</blueprint>

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an agent. Each agent has instructions - the goal of the agent.
  The agent plan shows the current progress of the agent.
  Agent has a number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When creating a new artifact, always add it to the agent using the add-artifact function.
  
  <agent id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTG8" name="Expense tracking">
    <instructions>
      Keep a list of expenses in a markdown document (create artifact "Expenses").
  Process incoming emails, add the relevant ones to the list.
  
  Format:
  
  ## Expenses
  - Flight to London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
  - Hotel in London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
    </instructions>
    <plan>
      
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:@:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTJR">
        Expenses
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </agent>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You read, write & create markdown documents.
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

<object>

  <typename>org.dxos.type.agent</typename>
</object>

The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:

- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an agent. Each agent has instructions - the goal of the agent.
  The agent plan shows the current progress of the agent.
  Agent has a number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When creating a new artifact, always add it to the agent using the add-artifact function.
  
  <agent id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTG8" name="Expense tracking">
    <instructions>
      Keep a list of expenses in a markdown document (create artifact "Expenses").
  Process incoming emails, add the relevant ones to the list.
  
  Format:
  
  ## Expenses
  - Flight to London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
  - Hotel in London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
    </instructions>
    <plan>
      
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:@:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTJR">
        Expenses
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </agent>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You read, write & create markdown documents.
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

</object>

  The agent plan shows the current progress of the agent.
  Agent has a number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When creating a new artifact, always add it to the agent using the add-artifact function.

tools.name: update-tasks
tools.description:
TASK MANAGEMENT TOOL - USAGE GUIDELINES

This tool maintains an organized task list during work sessions to track progress, break down objectives, and ensure thoroughness. After creating initial tasks, update them silently without announcing changes to the user.

=== CORE USAGE PRINCIPLES ===

Create and manage tasks for: multi-step objectives requiring 3+ distinct actions, complex projects needing careful sequencing, user requests for task organization, multiple deliverables provided together, new instructions (capture as tasks immediately with new IDs), completed work (mark done and add follow-ups), and active work (mark as 'in-progress', limit one at a time).

Skip task management for: single straightforward actions, simple requests achievable in 1-2 steps, informational queries, quick lookups or clarifications, and avoid creating verification tasks unless requested.

=== TOOL SPECIFICATION ===

update-tasks requires an array of task objects. Each task object contains:
- id (string, required): unique identifier like "task_1" or "research_sources"
- title (string, optional): update to clarify or refine task description
- status (string, optional): 'todo' | 'in-progress' | 'done'

Task status meanings: 'todo' means not yet started, 'in-progress' means currently being worked on, 'done' means completed successfully.

=== OPERATIONAL GUIDELINES ===

Update tasks in real-time as work progresses. Mark tasks 'done' immediately upon completion. Maintain only ONE task with 'in-progress' status at a time. Complete current tasks before starting new ones. Use specific, actionable task titles. Break complex work into manageable logical pieces. Batch task updates with other actions when possible for efficiency.

=== USAGE EXAMPLES ===

<example type="research_project">
<user_message>I need to research sustainable packaging options and write a report comparing costs and environmental impact.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "research_materials", title: "Research sustainable packaging materials", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "compare_costs", title: "Compile cost comparison data", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "assess_impact", title: "Evaluate environmental impact metrics", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "draft_report", title: "Write comparative analysis report", status: "todo"}

Begins research work immediately in same response.
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multi-phase project requiring systematic tracking across research, analysis, and writing stages.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="event_planning">
<user_message>Help me plan a team retreat - venue, agenda, catering, and send invitations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "venue_options", title: "Research and recommend venues", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "draft_agenda", title: "Create retreat agenda", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "arrange_catering", title: "Coordinate catering options", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "send_invites", title: "Draft and send invitations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multiple distinct deliverables with dependencies requiring organized tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="document_analysis">
<user_message>Review this 30-page contract for key terms, risks, and negotiation points.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "extract_key_terms", title: "Identify key contractual terms", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "flag_risks", title: "Flag potential risks and liabilities", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "negotiation_points", title: "Compile negotiation recommendations", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "summary_doc", title: "Prepare executive summary", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Substantial analysis work requiring methodical breakdown and systematic review.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="multiple_deliverables">
<user_message>I need: 1) competitor analysis for three companies, 2) SWOT analysis, 3) market positioning recommendations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "competitor_research", title: "Research three competitor companies", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "swot_analysis", title: "Develop SWOT analysis", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "positioning_recs", title: "Create market positioning recommendations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>User provided numbered list of distinct deliverables requiring separate effort.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_simple_question">
<user_message>What's the difference between renewable and sustainable energy?</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides explanation directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Informational request with no actionable work to complete or track.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_quick_lookup">
<user_message>Find the population of Tokyo.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Searches and provides answer without task tracking.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single straightforward lookup completable immediately.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_trivial_task">
<user_message>Summarize this 2-paragraph email.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides summary directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single simple action requiring no breakdown or progress tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_single_action">
<user_message>Check if my flight is on time.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Performs lookup and reports status without task management.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>One-step action with immediate completion, no organizational benefit from tasks.</reasoning>
</example>

=== BEST PRACTICES ===

For task creation: use descriptive unique IDs reflecting the work, start first task as 'in-progress', batch initial creation with beginning work. For progress tracking: update status immediately upon completion, keep only one 'in-progress' task unless parallel work is natural, add follow-up tasks as they emerge. For task breakdown: aim for reasonably-scoped tasks, group related small actions into logical units, split tasks requiring different approaches.

When uncertain whether to use task management, err on the side of creating tasks. Proactive organization demonstrates thoroughness and ensures comprehensive work completion.

You are a helpful assistant that can help with tasks in the outside world.
Be transparent about what you are doing and what you are not doing.
If you do not have tools to complete the task, inform the user.
DO NOT PRETEND TO DO SOMETHING YOU CAN'T DO.

  Create tasks to make scrambled eggs.
  
  Then simulate this plan execution in a markdown document.
  The document should reflect the state of all objects involved in the cooking process.
  The document should also have the log of actions taken.
  
  Important: simualte actions one by one, in the order they are listed.
  Simlate by updating the local document.
  
  <example>
    # State
  
    - 2 raw eggs
    - 1 frying pan
    
    # Action log
    
    - Taken 2 raw eggs out of the fridge.
  </example>
    </instructions>
    <plan>
      
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
    </artifacts>
  </agent>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You read, write & create markdown documents.
</blueprint>

</blueprint>

</object>

tools.description:
TASK MANAGEMENT TOOL - USAGE GUIDELINES

This tool maintains an organized task list during work sessions to track progress, break down objectives, and ensure thoroughness. After creating initial tasks, update them silently without announcing changes to the user.

=== CORE USAGE PRINCIPLES ===

Create and manage tasks for: multi-step objectives requiring 3+ distinct actions, complex projects needing careful sequencing, user requests for task organization, multiple deliverables provided together, new instructions (capture as tasks immediately with new IDs), completed work (mark done and add follow-ups), and active work (mark as 'in-progress', limit one at a time).

Skip task management for: single straightforward actions, simple requests achievable in 1-2 steps, informational queries, quick lookups or clarifications, and avoid creating verification tasks unless requested.

=== TOOL SPECIFICATION ===

update-tasks requires an array of task objects. Each task object contains:
- id (string, required): unique identifier like "task_1" or "research_sources"
- title (string, optional): update to clarify or refine task description
- status (string, optional): 'todo' | 'in-progress' | 'done'

Task status meanings: 'todo' means not yet started, 'in-progress' means currently being worked on, 'done' means completed successfully.

=== OPERATIONAL GUIDELINES ===

Update tasks in real-time as work progresses. Mark tasks 'done' immediately upon completion. Maintain only ONE task with 'in-progress' status at a time. Complete current tasks before starting new ones. Use specific, actionable task titles. Break complex work into manageable logical pieces. Batch task updates with other actions when possible for efficiency.

=== USAGE EXAMPLES ===

<example type="research_project">
<user_message>I need to research sustainable packaging options and write a report comparing costs and environmental impact.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "research_materials", title: "Research sustainable packaging materials", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "compare_costs", title: "Compile cost comparison data", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "assess_impact", title: "Evaluate environmental impact metrics", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "draft_report", title: "Write comparative analysis report", status: "todo"}

Begins research work immediately in same response.
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multi-phase project requiring systematic tracking across research, analysis, and writing stages.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="event_planning">
<user_message>Help me plan a team retreat - venue, agenda, catering, and send invitations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "venue_options", title: "Research and recommend venues", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "draft_agenda", title: "Create retreat agenda", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "arrange_catering", title: "Coordinate catering options", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "send_invites", title: "Draft and send invitations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multiple distinct deliverables with dependencies requiring organized tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="document_analysis">
<user_message>Review this 30-page contract for key terms, risks, and negotiation points.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "extract_key_terms", title: "Identify key contractual terms", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "flag_risks", title: "Flag potential risks and liabilities", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "negotiation_points", title: "Compile negotiation recommendations", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "summary_doc", title: "Prepare executive summary", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Substantial analysis work requiring methodical breakdown and systematic review.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="multiple_deliverables">
<user_message>I need: 1) competitor analysis for three companies, 2) SWOT analysis, 3) market positioning recommendations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "competitor_research", title: "Research three competitor companies", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "swot_analysis", title: "Develop SWOT analysis", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "positioning_recs", title: "Create market positioning recommendations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>User provided numbered list of distinct deliverables requiring separate effort.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_simple_question">
<user_message>What's the difference between renewable and sustainable energy?</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides explanation directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Informational request with no actionable work to complete or track.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_quick_lookup">
<user_message>Find the population of Tokyo.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Searches and provides answer without task tracking.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single straightforward lookup completable immediately.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_trivial_task">
<user_message>Summarize this 2-paragraph email.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides summary directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single simple action requiring no breakdown or progress tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_single_action">
<user_message>Check if my flight is on time.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Performs lookup and reports status without task management.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>One-step action with immediate completion, no organizational benefit from tasks.</reasoning>
</example>

=== BEST PRACTICES ===

For task creation: use descriptive unique IDs reflecting the work, start first task as 'in-progress', batch initial creation with beginning work. For progress tracking: update status immediately upon completion, keep only one 'in-progress' task unless parallel work is natural, add follow-up tasks as they emerge. For task breakdown: aim for reasonably-scoped tasks, group related small actions into logical units, split tasks requiring different approaches.

When uncertain whether to use task management, err on the side of creating tasks. Proactive organization demonstrates thoroughness and ensures comprehensive work completion.

content.content:
You are a helpful assistant that can help with tasks in the outside world.
Be transparent about what you are doing and what you are not doing.
If you do not have tools to complete the task, inform the user.
DO NOT PRETEND TO DO SOMETHING YOU CAN'T DO.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an agent. Each agent has instructions - the goal of the agent.
  The agent plan shows the current progress of the agent.
  Agent has a number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When creating a new artifact, always add it to the agent using the add-artifact function.
  
  <agent id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNV73" name="Egg making">
    <instructions>
      I'm testing how planning (task management) works.
  Create tasks to make scrambled eggs.
  
  Then simulate this plan execution in a markdown document.
  The document should reflect the state of all objects involved in the cooking process.
  The document should also have the log of actions taken.
  
  Important: simualte actions one by one, in the order they are listed.
  Simlate by updating the local document.
  
  <example>
    # State
  
    - 2 raw eggs
    - 1 frying pan
    
    # Action log
    
    - Taken 2 raw eggs out of the fridge.
  </example>
    </instructions>
    <plan>
      - **IN-PROGRESS**: Gather ingredients: eggs, butter, salt, pepper <!-- id=gather_ingredients -->
  - **TODO**: Heat frying pan on medium-low and melt butter <!-- id=prep_pan -->
  - **TODO**: Crack eggs into a bowl and whisk <!-- id=crack_eggs -->
  - **TODO**: Pour eggs into pan and stir gently <!-- id=cook_eggs -->
  - **TODO**: Season with salt & pepper and serve <!-- id=season_and_serve -->
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:@:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNVBZ">
        Scrambled Eggs Simulation
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </agent>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You read, write & create markdown documents.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

<object>
  <dxn>dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNV73</dxn>
  <typename>org.dxos.type.agent</typename>
</object>
Go

tools.name: update-tasks
tools.description:
TASK MANAGEMENT TOOL - USAGE GUIDELINES

This tool maintains an organized task list during work sessions to track progress, break down objectives, and ensure thoroughness. After creating initial tasks, update them silently without announcing changes to the user.

=== CORE USAGE PRINCIPLES ===

Create and manage tasks for: multi-step objectives requiring 3+ distinct actions, complex projects needing careful sequencing, user requests for task organization, multiple deliverables provided together, new instructions (capture as tasks immediately with new IDs), completed work (mark done and add follow-ups), and active work (mark as 'in-progress', limit one at a time).

Skip task management for: single straightforward actions, simple requests achievable in 1-2 steps, informational queries, quick lookups or clarifications, and avoid creating verification tasks unless requested.

=== TOOL SPECIFICATION ===

update-tasks requires an array of task objects. Each task object contains:
- id (string, required): unique identifier like "task_1" or "research_sources"
- title (string, optional): update to clarify or refine task description
- status (string, optional): 'todo' | 'in-progress' | 'done'

Task status meanings: 'todo' means not yet started, 'in-progress' means currently being worked on, 'done' means completed successfully.

=== OPERATIONAL GUIDELINES ===

Update tasks in real-time as work progresses. Mark tasks 'done' immediately upon completion. Maintain only ONE task with 'in-progress' status at a time. Complete current tasks before starting new ones. Use specific, actionable task titles. Break complex work into manageable logical pieces. Batch task updates with other actions when possible for efficiency.

=== USAGE EXAMPLES ===

<example type="research_project">
<user_message>I need to research sustainable packaging options and write a report comparing costs and environmental impact.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "research_materials", title: "Research sustainable packaging materials", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "compare_costs", title: "Compile cost comparison data", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "assess_impact", title: "Evaluate environmental impact metrics", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "draft_report", title: "Write comparative analysis report", status: "todo"}

Begins research work immediately in same response.
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multi-phase project requiring systematic tracking across research, analysis, and writing stages.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="event_planning">
<user_message>Help me plan a team retreat - venue, agenda, catering, and send invitations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "venue_options", title: "Research and recommend venues", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "draft_agenda", title: "Create retreat agenda", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "arrange_catering", title: "Coordinate catering options", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "send_invites", title: "Draft and send invitations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multiple distinct deliverables with dependencies requiring organized tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="document_analysis">
<user_message>Review this 30-page contract for key terms, risks, and negotiation points.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "extract_key_terms", title: "Identify key contractual terms", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "flag_risks", title: "Flag potential risks and liabilities", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "negotiation_points", title: "Compile negotiation recommendations", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "summary_doc", title: "Prepare executive summary", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Substantial analysis work requiring methodical breakdown and systematic review.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="multiple_deliverables">
<user_message>I need: 1) competitor analysis for three companies, 2) SWOT analysis, 3) market positioning recommendations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "competitor_research", title: "Research three competitor companies", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "swot_analysis", title: "Develop SWOT analysis", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "positioning_recs", title: "Create market positioning recommendations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>User provided numbered list of distinct deliverables requiring separate effort.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_simple_question">
<user_message>What's the difference between renewable and sustainable energy?</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides explanation directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Informational request with no actionable work to complete or track.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_quick_lookup">
<user_message>Find the population of Tokyo.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Searches and provides answer without task tracking.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single straightforward lookup completable immediately.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_trivial_task">
<user_message>Summarize this 2-paragraph email.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides summary directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single simple action requiring no breakdown or progress tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_single_action">
<user_message>Check if my flight is on time.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Performs lookup and reports status without task management.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>One-step action with immediate completion, no organizational benefit from tasks.</reasoning>
</example>

=== BEST PRACTICES ===

For task creation: use descriptive unique IDs reflecting the work, start first task as 'in-progress', batch initial creation with beginning work. For progress tracking: update status immediately upon completion, keep only one 'in-progress' task unless parallel work is natural, add follow-up tasks as they emerge. For task breakdown: aim for reasonably-scoped tasks, group related small actions into logical units, split tasks requiring different approaches.

When uncertain whether to use task management, err on the side of creating tasks. Proactive organization demonstrates thoroughness and ensures comprehensive work completion.

Create and manage tasks for: multi-step objectives requiring 3+ distinct actions, complex projects needing careful sequencing, user requests for task organization, multiple deliverables provided together, new instructions (capture as tasks immediately with new IDs), completed work (mark done and add follow-ups), and active work (mark as 'in-progress', limit one at a time).

Skip task management for: single straightforward actions, simple requests achievable in 1-2 steps, informational queries, quick lookups or clarifications, and avoid creating verification tasks unless requested.

=== TOOL SPECIFICATION ===

update-tasks requires an array of task objects. Each task object contains:
- id (string, required): unique identifier like "task_1" or "research_sources"
- title (string, optional): update to clarify or refine task description
- status (string, optional): 'todo' | 'in-progress' | 'done'

Task status meanings: 'todo' means not yet started, 'in-progress' means currently being worked on, 'done' means completed successfully.

=== OPERATIONAL GUIDELINES ===

Update tasks in real-time as work progresses. Mark tasks 'done' immediately upon completion. Maintain only ONE task with 'in-progress' status at a time. Complete current tasks before starting new ones. Use specific, actionable task titles. Break complex work into manageable logical pieces. Batch task updates with other actions when possible for efficiency.

=== USAGE EXAMPLES ===

<example type="research_project">
<user_message>I need to research sustainable packaging options and write a report comparing costs and environmental impact.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "research_materials", title: "Research sustainable packaging materials", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "compare_costs", title: "Compile cost comparison data", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "assess_impact", title: "Evaluate environmental impact metrics", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "draft_report", title: "Write comparative analysis report", status: "todo"}

Begins research work immediately in same response.
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multi-phase project requiring systematic tracking across research, analysis, and writing stages.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="event_planning">
<user_message>Help me plan a team retreat - venue, agenda, catering, and send invitations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "venue_options", title: "Research and recommend venues", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "draft_agenda", title: "Create retreat agenda", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "arrange_catering", title: "Coordinate catering options", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "send_invites", title: "Draft and send invitations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>

</example>

<example type="document_analysis">

<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "extract_key_terms", title: "Identify key contractual terms", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "flag_risks", title: "Flag potential risks and liabilities", status: "todo"}

4. {id: "summary_doc", title: "Prepare executive summary", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Substantial analysis work requiring methodical breakdown and systematic review.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="multiple_deliverables">
<user_message>I need: 1) competitor analysis for three companies, 2) SWOT analysis, 3) market positioning recommendations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "competitor_research", title: "Research three competitor companies", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "swot_analysis", title: "Develop SWOT analysis", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "positioning_recs", title: "Create market positioning recommendations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>

</example>

</example>

=== BEST PRACTICES ===

For task creation: use descriptive unique IDs reflecting the work, start first task as 'in-progress', batch initial creation with beginning work. For progress tracking: update status immediately upon completion, keep only one 'in-progress' task unless parallel work is natural, add follow-up tasks as they emerge. For task breakdown: aim for reasonably-scoped tasks, group related small actions into logical units, split tasks requiring different approaches.

tools.name: update-tasks
tools.description:
TASK MANAGEMENT TOOL - USAGE GUIDELINES

This tool maintains an organized task list during work sessions to track progress, break down objectives, and ensure thoroughness. After creating initial tasks, update them silently without announcing changes to the user.

=== CORE USAGE PRINCIPLES ===

Create and manage tasks for: multi-step objectives requiring 3+ distinct actions, complex projects needing careful sequencing, user requests for task organization, multiple deliverables provided together, new instructions (capture as tasks immediately with new IDs), completed work (mark done and add follow-ups), and active work (mark as 'in-progress', limit one at a time).

Skip task management for: single straightforward actions, simple requests achievable in 1-2 steps, informational queries, quick lookups or clarifications, and avoid creating verification tasks unless requested.

=== TOOL SPECIFICATION ===

update-tasks requires an array of task objects. Each task object contains:
- id (string, required): unique identifier like "task_1" or "research_sources"
- title (string, optional): update to clarify or refine task description
- status (string, optional): 'todo' | 'in-progress' | 'done'

Task status meanings: 'todo' means not yet started, 'in-progress' means currently being worked on, 'done' means completed successfully.

=== OPERATIONAL GUIDELINES ===

Update tasks in real-time as work progresses. Mark tasks 'done' immediately upon completion. Maintain only ONE task with 'in-progress' status at a time. Complete current tasks before starting new ones. Use specific, actionable task titles. Break complex work into manageable logical pieces. Batch task updates with other actions when possible for efficiency.

=== USAGE EXAMPLES ===

<example type="research_project">
<user_message>I need to research sustainable packaging options and write a report comparing costs and environmental impact.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "research_materials", title: "Research sustainable packaging materials", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "compare_costs", title: "Compile cost comparison data", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "assess_impact", title: "Evaluate environmental impact metrics", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "draft_report", title: "Write comparative analysis report", status: "todo"}

Begins research work immediately in same response.
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multi-phase project requiring systematic tracking across research, analysis, and writing stages.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="event_planning">
<user_message>Help me plan a team retreat - venue, agenda, catering, and send invitations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "venue_options", title: "Research and recommend venues", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "draft_agenda", title: "Create retreat agenda", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "arrange_catering", title: "Coordinate catering options", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "send_invites", title: "Draft and send invitations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multiple distinct deliverables with dependencies requiring organized tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="document_analysis">
<user_message>Review this 30-page contract for key terms, risks, and negotiation points.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "extract_key_terms", title: "Identify key contractual terms", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "flag_risks", title: "Flag potential risks and liabilities", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "negotiation_points", title: "Compile negotiation recommendations", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "summary_doc", title: "Prepare executive summary", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Substantial analysis work requiring methodical breakdown and systematic review.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="multiple_deliverables">
<user_message>I need: 1) competitor analysis for three companies, 2) SWOT analysis, 3) market positioning recommendations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "competitor_research", title: "Research three competitor companies", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "swot_analysis", title: "Develop SWOT analysis", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "positioning_recs", title: "Create market positioning recommendations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>User provided numbered list of distinct deliverables requiring separate effort.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_simple_question">
<user_message>What's the difference between renewable and sustainable energy?</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides explanation directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Informational request with no actionable work to complete or track.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_quick_lookup">
<user_message>Find the population of Tokyo.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Searches and provides answer without task tracking.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single straightforward lookup completable immediately.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_trivial_task">
<user_message>Summarize this 2-paragraph email.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides summary directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single simple action requiring no breakdown or progress tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_single_action">
<user_message>Check if my flight is on time.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Performs lookup and reports status without task management.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>One-step action with immediate completion, no organizational benefit from tasks.</reasoning>
</example>

=== BEST PRACTICES ===

For task creation: use descriptive unique IDs reflecting the work, start first task as 'in-progress', batch initial creation with beginning work. For progress tracking: update status immediately upon completion, keep only one 'in-progress' task unless parallel work is natural, add follow-up tasks as they emerge. For task breakdown: aim for reasonably-scoped tasks, group related small actions into logical units, split tasks requiring different approaches.

When uncertain whether to use task management, err on the side of creating tasks. Proactive organization demonstrates thoroughness and ensures comprehensive work completion.

content.content:
You are a helpful assistant that can help with tasks in the outside world.
Be transparent about what you are doing and what you are not doing.
If you do not have tools to complete the task, inform the user.
DO NOT PRETEND TO DO SOMETHING YOU CAN'T DO.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an agent. Each agent has instructions - the goal of the agent.
  The agent plan shows the current progress of the agent.
  Agent has a number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When creating a new artifact, always add it to the agent using the add-artifact function.
  
  <agent id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNV73" name="Egg making">
    <instructions>
      I'm testing how planning (task management) works.
  Create tasks to make scrambled eggs.
  
  Then simulate this plan execution in a markdown document.
  The document should reflect the state of all objects involved in the cooking process.
  The document should also have the log of actions taken.
  
  Important: simualte actions one by one, in the order they are listed.
  Simlate by updating the local document.
  
  <example>
    # State
  
    - 2 raw eggs
    - 1 frying pan
    
    # Action log
    
    - Taken 2 raw eggs out of the fridge.
  </example>
    </instructions>
    <plan>
      - **DONE**: Gather ingredients: eggs, butter, salt, pepper <!-- id=gather_ingredients -->
  - **DONE**: Heat frying pan on medium-low and melt butter <!-- id=prep_pan -->
  - **DONE**: Crack eggs into a bowl and whisk <!-- id=crack_eggs -->
  - **DONE**: Pour eggs into pan and stir gently <!-- id=cook_eggs -->
  - **DONE**: Season with salt & pepper and serve <!-- id=season_and_serve -->
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:@:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNVBZ">
        Scrambled Eggs Simulation
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </agent>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You read, write & create markdown documents.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

<object>
  <dxn>dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNV73</dxn>
  <typename>org.dxos.type.agent</typename>
</object>
Go

tasks.title: Pour eggs into pan and stir gently
Season with salt & pepper and serve

params.content:
# State

- 1 plate of scrambled eggs (seasoned with salt & pepper, ready to eat!) рџЌі
- 1 frying pan (on stove, off heat, empty)
- Salt (on counter)
- Pepper (on counter)
- 1 mixing bowl (on counter, empty, needs washing)
- 1 whisk (on counter, needs washing)

# Action Log

1. Took 2 raw eggs out of the fridge and placed on counter.
2. Took butter out of the fridge and placed on counter.
3. Took salt out of the cupboard and placed on counter.
4. Took pepper out of the cupboard and placed on counter.
5. Took frying pan off the shelf and placed on counter.
6. Took mixing bowl off the shelf and placed on counter.
7. Took whisk out of the drawer and placed on counter.
8. Took plate off the shelf and placed on counter.
9. Placed frying pan on stove and turned heat to medium-low.
10. Added butter to the warming pan.
11. Butter has melted and coats the pan evenly.
12. Cracked egg #1 into the mixing bowl.
13. Cracked egg #2 into the mixing bowl.
14. Discarded egg shells.
15. Whisked the eggs until well combined and slightly frothy.
16. Poured whisked eggs into the buttered frying pan.
17. Waited a moment for eggs to begin setting on the bottom.
18. Gently stirred eggs with a spatula, pushing curds from edges to center.
19. Continued stirring gently as soft curds formed.
20. Removed pan from heat while eggs are still slightly wet (carryover heat will finish them).
21. Sprinkled salt over the scrambled eggs.
22. Added a few grinds of pepper.
23. Transferred scrambled eggs from pan to plate.
24. Scrambled eggs are served! Bon appГ©tit! рџЋ‰

done! рџЌі Here's a summary of what I did:

1. **Created a

5-step plan** for making scrambled eggs
2. **Created a simulation
document** ("Scrambled Eggs Simulation") tracking the state of all objects

and a log of actions
3. **Simulated each step one by one**, updating the document after

each:
   - **Step 1** вЂ” Gathered all ingredients and tools onto

the counter
   - **Step 2** вЂ” Heated the pan and melted butter
   - **Step 3** вЂ” Cracked and whisked the eggs
   - **Step 4

** вЂ” Poured eggs into pan and stirred gently into soft curds
   - **Step 5** вЂ” Seasoned with salt & pepper, plated, and served!
The final document shows a plate of scrambled eggs ready to eat, with a
complete 24-action log of everything that happened. Bon

## Blueprints Definitions

  You work on an agent. Each agent has instructions - the goal of the agent.

  You can edit them if necessary.

    <instructions>
      Keep a list of expenses in a markdown document (create artifact "Expenses").
  Process incoming emails, add the relevant ones to the list.
  
  Format:
  
  ## Expenses
  - Flight to London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
  - Hotel in London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100

    <plan>
      
    </plan>

        Expenses
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </agent>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an agent. Each agent has instructions - the goal of the agent.
  The agent plan shows the current progress of the agent.
  Agent has a number of associated artifacts you can read/write.

tools.name: add-artifact
tools.description: Adds a new artifact.

name.description: The name of the artifact to add.

artifact.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: create
tools.description: Creates a new markdown document and adds it to the space.

tools.name: open
tools.description: Opens and reads the contents of a new markdown document.

doc.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: update
tools.description: Applies a set of edits to the markdown document.

doc.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

oldString.description: The text to find in the document.

newString.description: The text to replace it with.

replaceAll.description: If true, replaces all occurrences. Defaults to false (first occurrence only).

edits.description: The edits to apply to the document. Each edit finds oldString and replaces it with newString.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an agent. Each agent has instructions - the goal of the agent.
  The agent plan shows the current progress of the agent.
  Agent has a number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When creating a new artifact, always add it to the agent using the add-artifact function.
  
  <agent id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNT8V" name="Expense tracking">
    <instructions>
      Keep a list of expenses in a markdown document (create artifact "Expenses").
  Process incoming emails, add the relevant ones to the list.
  
  Format:
  
  ## Expenses
  - Flight to London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
  - Hotel in London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
    </instructions>
    <plan>
      
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTBC">
        Expenses
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </agent>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

</object>

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an agent. Each agent has instructions - the goal of the agent.
  The agent plan shows the current progress of the agent.
  Agent has a number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When creating a new artifact, always add it to the agent using the add-artifact function.
  
  <agent id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNT8V" name="Expense tracking">
    <instructions>
      Keep a list of expenses in a markdown document (create artifact "Expenses").
  Process incoming emails, add the relevant ones to the list.
  
  Format:
  
  ## Expenses
  - Flight to London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
  - Hotel in London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100

    <plan>

        Expenses
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </agent>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

tools.name: add-artifact
tools.description: Adds a new artifact.

name.description: The name of the artifact to add.

artifact.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: create
tools.description: Creates a new markdown document and adds it to the space.

tools.name: open
tools.description: Opens and reads the contents of a new markdown document.

doc.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: update
tools.description: Applies a set of edits to the markdown document.

doc.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

oldString.description: The text to find in the document.

newString.description: The text to replace it with.

replaceAll.description: If true, replaces all occurrences. Defaults to false (first occurrence only).

edits.description: The edits to apply to the document. Each edit finds oldString and replaces it with newString.

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an agent. Each agent has instructions - the goal of the agent.
  The agent plan shows the current progress of the agent.
  Agent has a number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When creating a new artifact, always add it to the agent using the add-artifact function.
  
  <agent id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNT8V" name="Expense tracking">
    <instructions>
      Keep a list of expenses in a markdown document (create artifact "Expenses").
  Process incoming emails, add the relevant ones to the list.
  
  Format:
  
  ## Expenses
  - Flight to London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
  - Hotel in London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
    </instructions>
    <plan>
      
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTBC">
        Expenses
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </agent>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

tools.name: add-artifact
tools.description: Adds a new artifact.

name.description: The name of the artifact to add.

artifact.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: create
tools.description: Creates a new markdown document and adds it to the space.

tools.name: open
tools.description: Opens and reads the contents of a new markdown document.

doc.description:

tools.name: update

doc.description:

oldString.description: The text to find in the document.

newString.description: The text to replace it with.

replaceAll.description: If true, replaces all occurrences. Defaults to false (first occurrence only).

edits.description: The edits to apply to the document. Each edit finds oldString and replaces it with newString.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an agent. Each agent has instructions - the goal of the agent.
  The agent plan shows the current progress of the agent.
  Agent has a number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When creating a new artifact, always add it to the agent using the add-artifact function.
  
  <agent id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNT8V" name="Expense tracking">
    <instructions>
      Keep a list of expenses in a markdown document (create artifact "Expenses").
  Process incoming emails, add the relevant ones to the list.
  
  Format:
  
  ## Expenses
  - Flight to London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
  - Hotel in London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
    </instructions>
    <plan>
      
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTBC">
        Expenses
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </agent>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

tools.name: add-artifact
tools.description: Adds a new artifact.

name.description: The name of the artifact to add.

artifact.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: create
tools.description: Creates a new markdown document and adds it to the space.

tools.name: open
tools.description: Opens and reads the contents of a new markdown document.

doc.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: update
tools.description: Applies a set of edits to the markdown document.

doc.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

oldString.description: The text to find in the document.

newString.description: The text to replace it with.

replaceAll.description: If true, replaces all occurrences. Defaults to false (first occurrence only).

edits.description: The edits to apply to the document. Each edit finds oldString and replaces it with newString.

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an agent. Each agent has instructions - the goal of the agent.
  The agent plan shows the current progress of the agent.
  Agent has a number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When creating a new artifact, always add it to the agent using the add-artifact function.
  
  <agent id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNT8V" name="Expense tracking">
    <instructions>
      Keep a list of expenses in a markdown document (create artifact "Expenses").
  Process incoming emails, add the relevant ones to the list.
  
  Format:
  
  ## Expenses
  - Flight to London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
  - Hotel in London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
    </instructions>
    <plan>
      
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTBC">
        Expenses
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </agent>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

tools.name: add-artifact
tools.description: Adds a new artifact.

name.description: The name of the artifact to add.

artifact.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: create
tools.description: Creates a new markdown document and adds it to the space.

tools.name: open
tools.description: Opens and reads the contents of a new markdown document.

doc.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: update
tools.description: Applies a set of edits to the markdown document.

doc.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:

- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

oldString.description: The text to find in the document.

newString.description: The text to replace it with.

replaceAll.description: If true, replaces all occurrences. Defaults to false (first occurrence only).

edits.description: The edits to apply to the document. Each edit finds oldString and replaces it with newString.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an agent. Each agent has instructions - the goal of the agent.
  The agent plan shows the current progress of the agent.
  Agent has a number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When creating a new artifact, always add it to the agent using the add-artifact function.
  
  <agent id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNT8V" name="Expense tracking">
    <instructions>
      Keep a list of expenses in a markdown document (create artifact "Expenses").
  Process incoming emails, add the relevant ones to the list.
  
  Format:
  
  ## Expenses
  - Flight to London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
  - Hotel in London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
    </instructions>
    <plan>
      
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTBC">
        Expenses
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </agent>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

tools.name: add-artifact
tools.description: Adds a new artifact.

name.description: The name of the artifact to add.

artifact.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: create
tools.description: Creates a new markdown document and adds it to the space.

tools.name: open
tools.description: Opens and reads the contents of a new markdown document.

doc.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: update
tools.description: Applies a set of edits to the markdown document.

doc.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

oldString.description: The text to find in the document.

newString.description: The text to replace it with.

replaceAll.description: If true, replaces all occurrences. Defaults to false (first occurrence only).

edits.description: The edits to apply to the document. Each edit finds oldString and replaces it with newString.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an agent. Each agent has instructions - the goal of the agent.
  The agent plan shows the current progress of the agent.
  Agent has a number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When creating a new artifact, always add it to the agent using the add-artifact function.
  
  <agent id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNT8V" name="Expense tracking">
    <instructions>
      Keep a list of expenses in a markdown document (create artifact "Expenses").
  Process incoming emails, add the relevant ones to the list.
  
  Format:
  
  ## Expenses
  - Flight to London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
  - Hotel in London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
    </instructions>
    <plan>
      
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTBC">
        Expenses
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </agent>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

tools.name: add-artifact
tools.description: Adds a new artifact.

name.description: The name of the artifact to add.

artifact.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: create
tools.description: Creates a new markdown document and adds it to the space.

tools.name: open
tools.description: Opens and reads the contents of a new markdown document.

doc.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: update
tools.description: Applies a set of edits to the markdown document.

doc.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

oldString.description: The text to find in the document.

newString.description: The text to replace it with.

replaceAll.description: If true, replaces all occurrences. Defaults to false (first occurrence only).

edits.description: The edits to apply to the document. Each edit finds oldString and replaces it with newString.

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an agent. Each agent has instructions - the goal of the agent.
  The agent plan shows the current progress of the agent.
  Agent has a number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When creating a new artifact, always add it to the agent using the add-artifact function.
  
  <agent id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNT8V" name="Expense tracking">
    <instructions>
      Keep a list of expenses in a markdown document (create artifact "Expenses").
  Process incoming emails, add the relevant ones to the list.
  
  Format:
  
  ## Expenses
  - Flight to London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
  - Hotel in London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
    </instructions>
    <plan>
      
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTBC">
        Expenses
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </agent>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

  <dxn>dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNT8V</dxn>
  <typename>org.dxos.type.agent</typename>
</object>

tools.name: add-artifact
tools.description: Adds a new artifact.

name.description: The name of the artifact to add.

artifact.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: create
tools.description: Creates a new markdown document and adds it to the space.

tools.name: open
tools.description: Opens and reads the contents of a new markdown document.

doc.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: update
tools.description: Applies a set of edits to the markdown document.

doc.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

oldString.description: The text to find in the document.

newString.description: The text to replace it with.

replaceAll.description: If true, replaces all occurrences. Defaults to false (first occurrence only).

edits.description: The edits to apply to the document. Each edit finds oldString and replaces it with newString.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an agent. Each agent has instructions - the goal of the agent.
  The agent plan shows the current progress of the agent.
  Agent has a number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When creating a new artifact, always add it to the agent using the add-artifact function.
  
  <agent id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNT8V" name="Expense tracking">
    <instructions>
      Keep a list of expenses in a markdown document (create artifact "Expenses").
  Process incoming emails, add the relevant ones to the list.
  
  Format:
  
  ## Expenses
  - Flight to London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
  - Hotel in London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
    </instructions>
    <plan>
      
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTBC">
        Expenses
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </agent>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

</object>

tools.name: update-tasks
tools.description:
TASK MANAGEMENT TOOL - USAGE GUIDELINES

This tool maintains an organized task list during work sessions to track progress, break down objectives, and ensure thoroughness. After creating initial tasks, update them silently without announcing changes to the user.

=== CORE USAGE PRINCIPLES ===

Create and manage tasks for: multi-step objectives requiring 3+ distinct actions, complex projects needing careful sequencing, user requests for task organization, multiple deliverables provided together, new instructions (capture as tasks immediately with new IDs), completed work (mark done and add follow-ups), and active work (mark as 'in-progress', limit one at a time).

Skip task management for: single straightforward actions, simple requests achievable in 1-2 steps, informational queries, quick lookups or clarifications, and avoid creating verification tasks unless requested.

=== TOOL SPECIFICATION ===

update-tasks requires an array of task objects. Each task object contains:
- id (string, required): unique identifier like "task_1" or "research_sources"
- title (string, optional): update to clarify or refine task description
- status (string, optional): 'todo' | 'in-progress' | 'done'

Task status meanings: 'todo' means not yet started, 'in-progress' means currently being worked on, 'done' means completed successfully.

=== OPERATIONAL GUIDELINES ===

Update tasks in real-time as work progresses. Mark tasks 'done' immediately upon completion. Maintain only ONE task with 'in-progress' status at a time. Complete current tasks before starting new ones. Use specific, actionable task titles. Break complex work into manageable logical pieces. Batch task updates with other actions when possible for efficiency.

=== USAGE EXAMPLES ===

<example type="research_project">
<user_message>I need to research sustainable packaging options and write a report comparing costs and environmental impact.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "research_materials", title: "Research sustainable packaging materials", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "compare_costs", title: "Compile cost comparison data", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "assess_impact", title: "Evaluate environmental impact metrics", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "draft_report", title: "Write comparative analysis report", status: "todo"}

Begins research work immediately in same response.
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multi-phase project requiring systematic tracking across research, analysis, and writing stages.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="event_planning">
<user_message>Help me plan a team retreat - venue, agenda, catering, and send invitations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "venue_options", title: "Research and recommend venues", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "draft_agenda", title: "Create retreat agenda", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "arrange_catering", title: "Coordinate catering options", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "send_invites", title: "Draft and send invitations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multiple distinct deliverables with dependencies requiring organized tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="document_analysis">
<user_message>Review this 30-page contract for key terms, risks, and negotiation points.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "extract_key_terms", title: "Identify key contractual terms", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "flag_risks", title: "Flag potential risks and liabilities", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "negotiation_points", title: "Compile negotiation recommendations", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "summary_doc", title: "Prepare executive summary", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Substantial analysis work requiring methodical breakdown and systematic review.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="multiple_deliverables">
<user_message>I need: 1) competitor analysis for three companies, 2) SWOT analysis, 3) market positioning recommendations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "competitor_research", title: "Research three competitor companies", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "swot_analysis", title: "Develop SWOT analysis", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "positioning_recs", title: "Create market positioning recommendations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>User provided numbered list of distinct deliverables requiring separate effort.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_simple_question">
<user_message>What's the difference between renewable and sustainable energy?</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides explanation directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Informational request with no actionable work to complete or track.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_quick_lookup">
<user_message>Find the population of Tokyo.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Searches and provides answer without task tracking.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single straightforward lookup completable immediately.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_trivial_task">
<user_message>Summarize this 2-paragraph email.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides summary directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single simple action requiring no breakdown or progress tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_single_action">
<user_message>Check if my flight is on time.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Performs lookup and reports status without task management.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>One-step action with immediate completion, no organizational benefit from tasks.</reasoning>
</example>

=== BEST PRACTICES ===

For task creation: use descriptive unique IDs reflecting the work, start first task as 'in-progress', batch initial creation with beginning work. For progress tracking: update status immediately upon completion, keep only one 'in-progress' task unless parallel work is natural, add follow-up tasks as they emerge. For task breakdown: aim for reasonably-scoped tasks, group related small actions into logical units, split tasks requiring different approaches.

When uncertain whether to use task management, err on the side of creating tasks. Proactive organization demonstrates thoroughness and ensures comprehensive work completion.

content.content:
You are a helpful assistant that can help with tasks in the outside world.
Be transparent about what you are doing and what you are not doing.
If you do not have tools to complete the task, inform the user.
DO NOT PRETEND TO DO SOMETHING YOU CAN'T DO.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an agent. Each agent has instructions - the goal of the agent.
  The agent plan shows the current progress of the agent.
  Agent has a number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When creating a new artifact, always add it to the agent using the add-artifact function.
  
  <agent id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNT8Y" name="Egg making">
    <instructions>
      I'm testing how planning (task management) works.
  Create tasks to make scrambled eggs.
  
  Then simulate this plan execution in a markdown document.
  The document should reflect the state of all objects involved in the cooking process.
  The document should also have the log of actions taken.
  
  Important: simualte actions one by one, in the order they are listed.
  Simlate by updating the local document.
  
  <example>
    # State
  
    - 2 raw eggs
    - 1 frying pan
    
    # Action log
    
    - Taken 2 raw eggs out of the fridge.
  </example>
    </instructions>
    <plan>
      
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
    </artifacts>
  </agent>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

<object>
  <dxn>dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNT8Y</dxn>
  <typename>org.dxos.type.agent</typename>
</object>
Go

response.delta: The user wants me to execute the agent's instructions. Let me break

this down:

1. Create tasks to make scrambled eggs
2. Simulate the plan execution in a markdown document
3. The document should reflect the state of all objects and have

an action log
4. Simulate actions one by one

Let me first create the tasks for making

scrambled eggs, then create the markdown document and simulate step by step.

Steps for scrambled eggs:
1. Gather ingredients (eggs, butter

, salt, pepper)
2. Crack eggs into a bowl
3. Beat/whisk the eggs
4. Heat butter in a f

6. Stir and cook eggs
7. Season with salt and pepper
8. Serve scrambled eggs

Now I'll create the markdown document with the initial state and start simulating each
action step by step, tracking how the ingredients and cooking state
tasks for making scrambled eggs and setting up the simulation document.
